title: Values and types

publication.changeSummary: Topic-specific draft for Values and types; requires human source, technical, pedagogy, and accessibility review. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: CS2023 Software Development Fundamentals; August 2023; section applicable to Values and types
sources.claim: A value's conceptual type determines which operations are meaningful, such as arithmetic for numbers, logical operations for truth values, and ordered character operations for text.

canonicalExplanation.summary: A value's conceptual type determines which operations are meaningful, such as arithmetic for numbers, logical operations for truth values, and ordered character operations for text.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: The text value 12 can be joined with kg to display 12kg, while the numeric value 12 can be added to 3 to produce 15. Your goal is to choose a suitable conceptual type and reject invalid operations for a value. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Pseudocode. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Operational model
sections.body: A value's conceptual type determines which operations are meaningful, such as arithmetic for numbers, logical operations for truth values, and ordered character operations for text. The learner should use this model to predict an observable result rather than merely repeat terminology.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure point
sections.body: Concrete languages differ in numeric ranges, coercion, representation, nullability, and collection types, so conceptual compatibility does not guarantee identical runtime behavior. A correct explanation therefore includes the applicable assumptions and avoids this common mistake: Values that look similar on screen always support the same operations, so text 12 and number 12 are interchangeable.

includes: Distinguish numbers, truth values, text and collections by valid operations.
Choose a suitable conceptual type and reject invalid operations for a value.
excludes: Concrete languages differ in numeric ranges, coercion, representation, nullability, and collection types, so conceptual compatibility does not guarantee identical runtime behavior.

outcomes: Choose a suitable conceptual type and reject invalid operations for a value.

examples.title: Values and types: direct case
examples.situation: The text value 12 can be joined with kg to display 12kg, while the numeric value 12 can be added to 3 to produce 15.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the starting evidence: The text value 12 can be joined with kg to display 12kg, while the numeric value 12 can be added to 3 to produce 15.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.

Step 3 — Check the result against this correction: Choose and validate a type from the intended operations; convert representations explicitly when moving between text, numbers, truth values, and collections.
examples.result: The case supports the declared outcome because Choose and validate a type from the intended operations; convert representations explicitly when moving between text, numbers, truth values, and collections.

examples.title: Values and types: boundary case
examples.situation: A truth value records whether access is allowed, while a collection records several permissions that must be searched or traversed.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed context: A truth value records whether access is allowed, while a collection records several permissions that must be searched or traversed.

Step 2 — Keep this boundary explicit: Concrete languages differ in numeric ranges, coercion, representation, nullability, and collection types, so conceptual compatibility does not guarantee identical runtime behavior.
Step 3 — Reject the documented misconception: Values that look similar on screen always support the same operations, so text 12 and number 12 are interchangeable.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Choose a suitable conceptual type and reject invalid operations for a value.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Values that look similar on screen always support the same operations, so text 12 and number 12 are interchangeable.
misconceptions.correction: Choose and validate a type from the intended operations; convert representations explicitly when moving between text, numbers, truth values, and collections.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using the direct and boundary scenarios, explain why this belief fails: Values that look similar on screen always support the same operations, so text 12 and number 12 are interchangeable.

analogy.example: A labeled scoreboard can model Values and types: each label exposes a current value and each recorded event produces the next visible state.
usefulFor: Reasoning about values, expressions, named state, and changes across time
limitations: The scoreboard does not accurately model binary representation, scope, aliases, object identity, or language coercion.
Concrete languages differ in numeric ranges, coercion, representation, nullability, and collection types, so conceptual compatibility does not guarantee identical runtime behavior.
